texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane distillation separates liquids and solutes using a hydrophobic microporous membrane. Di ff erent con fi gurations have been investigated at lab scale, among which direct contact membrane distillation (DCMD) and air gap membrane distillation (AGMD). Lab scale studies comparing di ff erent con fi gurations show higher fl ux for DCMD, while AGMD is more energy e ffi cient. However, no straightforward directions are given yet on how to translate these results to pilot scale. As membrane distillation is currently shifting from lab environment towards pilot experiments, a good understanding of pilot scale modules and the di ff erences with lab modules are important. In this study, DCMD and AGMD were compared both at lab and pilot scale using the same membrane. At lab scale, it was found that the fl ux of DCMD is a factor 4 higher compared to the fl ux of AGMD. However, at pilot scale, the AGMD modules showed a higher fl ux and lower energy consumption compared to DCMD. As it is important for further upscaling of the technology, this study focusses on the explanation behind these unexpected higher fl uxes of AGMD modules. Since the AGMD modules showed better performance and do not require an additional recuperating heat exchanger, this con fi guration is preferred over DCMD for larger scale applications.

texts.orig: Fig. 1. In DCMD the driving force is the interfacial temperature di ff erence over the membrane and the vapor transport distance is equal to the membrane. In AGMD, an additional isolating air layer strongly decreases the heat losses in this case. In an entirely dry gap, the thickness of the gap should be added to the membrane thickness to obtain the total vapor transport distance and therefore, also the vapor permeability is severely reduced by the gap. The driving force in this case is not the interfacial temperature di ff erence over the membrane, but the temperature di ff erence over the membrane and the gap. As shown by Hitsov et al. [4], up to 45% of the gap is fl ooded at pilot scale, which also should be accounted for in the interpretation of performance results.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane distillation is an emerging technology using a hydrophobic porous membrane to separate liquid and solutes, such as salts. Di ff erent con fi gurations are used to provide a driving force over the membrane [1,2]. The direct contact MD (DCMD) con fi guration is the most studied con fi guration at lab scale due to the simplicity in design and the high fl uxes. Because both hot and cold sides are in direct contact with the membrane, this con fi guration su ff ers more from heat loss due to conduction through the membrane. Additionally, an external heat exchanger is required for heat recuperation in DCMD. To reduce the heat loss due to conduction, an air gap is introduced between the membrane and a cold surface on permeate side in air-gap membrane distillation (AGMD). Because of the separation of coolant and permeate, the cold feed can be used as coolant. The cold feed is preheated in the module in counter current fl ow, whereby thermal energy is recuperated internally in the module [3].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Besides fl ux, also the thermal e ffi ciency is of major importance for the selection of a con fi guration. At lab scale, this e ffi ciency is evaluated based on the ratio of the energy consumed by fl ux and the total energy consumed after a single pass of the liquid through the module channel. At lab scale only few publications considered the energy consumption [7,10]. At full scale however, this single pass energy e ffi ciency is of minor importance, because both in DCMD and in AGMD, part of this energy is recuperated. Therefore at full scale, the gained output ratio (GOR) is used, which is de fi ned as the ratio of the energy used for evaporation and the total duty of the heat exchanger. Summers et al. concluded that with both con fi gurations high GORs are feasible based on simulations [15]. Winter showed that comparing a 8.7 m 2 AGMD module (l = 6.5 m) and a 9.8 m 2 DCMD (l = 7 m), the highest permeate output rate (l·h -1 ) and thermal energy e ffi ciency was obtained for AGMD [16]. In a previous work focusing on modelling of full scale AGMD modules, it was also shown that AGMD modules can have higher fl uxes than DCMD with equal channel length and membrane surface area [4]. These pilot scale observations of Winter et al. and Hitsov et al. are in contrast with the fi ndings at lab scale.

At lab scale, this e ffi ciency is evaluated based on the ratio of the energy consumed by fl ux and the total energy consumed after a single pass of the liquid through the module channel. At lab scale only few publications considered the energy consumption [7,10]. Therefore at full scale, the gained output ratio (GOR) is used, which is de fi ned as the ratio of the energy used for evaporation and the total duty of the heat exchanger. Summers et al. concluded that with both con fi gurations high GORs are feasible based on simulations [15]. Winter showed that comparing a 8.7 m 2 AGMD module (l = 6.5 m) and a 9.8 m 2 DCMD (l = 7 m), the highest permeate output rate (l·h -1 ) and thermal energy e ffi ciency was obtained for AGMD [16]. These pilot scale observations of Winter et al. and Hitsov et al. are in contrast with the fi ndings at lab scale.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To investigate and explain this di ff erence in MD performance between lab and full scale, this follow-up study focusses on the fl ux and energy e ffi ciency behavior of a lab scale (0.0108 m 2 ) and a pilot scale 7.2 m 2 DCMD and AGMD module. Both pilot scale modules were from the same provider (Aquastill) and have the same structure and dimensions. The membrane and spacers used in these modules are also equal to the ones used at lab scale making a direct comparison possible. To the best of our knowledge, this is the fi rst that time such a direct, indepth comparison is made between lab and pilot scale AGMD and DCMD. Di ff erent process conditions were used and the tests were compared with the results obtained from the lab scale testing, providing novel and surprising insights in the choice of a MD con fi guration. These insights can facilitate the transition from the small scale lab tests towards module design for industrial applications of MD. This topic is recently also indicated as a main focus area for MD by industry experts [17].

Both pilot scale modules were from the same provider (Aquastill) and have the same structure and dimensions. texts.text: 2.2.2. Pilot scale MD

texts.label: text
texts.orig: At pilot scale, the DCMD and AGMD con fi guration were compared. The pilot scale setup was delivered by Aquastill BV (The Netherlands). The MD pilot system can be equipped with multiple modules, as the connections can easily be changed (Fig. 4). The height of the module was 0.4 m and the channel length varied depending on the membrane area that was used. For a 7.2 m 2 module, the channel length is 1.5 m. For the 14.4 m 2 , the channel length equals 3 m. The module had 6 hot and 6 cold parallel channels, was arranged in counter current fl ow and uses the PE1 membrane.

The MD pilot system can be equipped with multiple modules, as the connections can easily be changed (Fig. 4). The height of the module was 0.4 m and the channel length varied depending on the membrane area that was used. For a 7.2 m 2 module, the channel length is 1.5 m. For the 14.4 m 2 , the channel length equals 3 m. The module had 6 hot and 6 cold parallel channels, was arranged in counter current fl ow and uses the PE1 membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 5 shows a simpli fi ed scheme of the arrangement of the MD pilot. For DCMD, the feed and coolant were circulated in two separate loops, of which the fl ow velocity could be adapted from 500 to 1500 L·h -1 . In the MD module and the heat exchangers, the fl ow was in counter-current mode. The DCMD mode used an additional heat exchanger for heat recuperation of 8 m 2 to allow for higher GOR. This fl ow rate was measured using fl ow rate meters (Bürkert 8032, Germany) and regulated by a controller. The temperatures were measured with PT-100 temperature sensors (Bürkert TR-30P, Germany). Based on these temperatures, the GOR was used to evaluate the energy consumption (Eq. (3)). The GOR is given by the ratio of the latent heat of evaporation ( Δ H) of the produced water (m) and the energy consumed by the system (Ethermal), which is equal to the duty of the heat exchanger. This duty is given by the temperature rise induced by the heat exchanger and can be calculated from the temperature di ff erence between the condenser outlet channel and the evaporator inlet channel Δ THX.

A pressure sensor (Bürkert 8314, Germany) measures the height in the permeate and feed tank, from which the volume and hence fl ux could be calculated. At a certain height level, a pump recirculated the distillate back into the feed tank, which enabled to work with constant feed concentration. Pressure sensors (Bürkert 8314, Germany) were added before and after the module to measure the pressure drop over the module and in the system.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the air gap MD modules, an aluminum foil was used to separate the coolant and the permeate channel. The aluminum foil and the membrane were separated by the gap spacer with a thickness of 0.8 mm. The system only used one circulation loop for this con fi guration, where the cold feed entered the module and heats up at the condenser side. From the outlet of the condenser side, the feed is directed towards the heating heat exchanger, after which it enters the evaporator side of the module. An additional outlet was provided to collect the permeate, which was drained by gravity. The P & ID of the DCMD and AGMD system is given in Appendix 1. Both the DCMD and AGMD system are simulated and explained in more detail in [4,21,22].

texts.orig: lab scale 7% of the total in fl ow energy is exchanged in the module for the AGMD and 21% is exchanged in the DCMD module. In contrast, at full scale 69% and 82% of the in fl ow energy is exchanged in the module for AGMD and DCMD respectively, showing the limitation of the in fl ow energy at full scale. As pointed out by the lab scale experiments, less exchanged energy only does not imply a higher fl ux. However, because AGMD is slightly more e ffi cient and AGMD is less limited by in fl ow energy, more energy is left to contribute to fl ux in the AGMD modules. The higher fl ux of AGMD should therefore not be attributed to the lower exchange energy, but rather to a combination of lower exchange energy end higher energy e ffi ciency. The temperature pro fi les at pilot scale are also simulated using the model developed in [4] for DCMD and AGMD (Fig. 9 A and B). This con fi rms the lower bulk temperature di ff erence over the module in DCMD. Additionally, at feed side the temperature at the membrane interface is reduced more severely in DCMD. Important to note is also that the temperature di ff erence at the membrane interface on feed and permeate side in DCMD is equal to the real driving force. In AGMD, the gap is fi lled with air and is also partially fl ooded [4]. In this case, the interfacial temperature di ff erence only represents the driving force for the fl ooded parts. In the remainder parts, the driving force for vapor transport is equal to the temperature di ff erence over the entire non- fl ooded gas gap (as explained in Fig. 1).

texts.orig: An important di ff erence between the lab scale and pilot scale test is the di ff erence in controlling the temperature. At lab scale, the feed inlet and permeate outlet are controlled, i.e., the temperature di ff erence over the membrane is set. At full scale, it is the feed inlet temperature and the permeate inlet temperature that are controlled. Due to the large module, variations in fl ux and conduction it is impossible to control the temperature di ff erence over the membrane. Therefore, the temperature di ff erence over the module is discussed in this paragraph. For the different process conditions, the logarithmic mean temperature di ff erence (LMTD) over the membrane was measured, varying between 4.6 and 10.1 °C for DCMD and 7.2 and 16.9 °C for AGMD. This temperature di ff erence is larger for AGMD, because less heat is lost due to condition. It was also found that the fl ux is slightly more sensitive to temperature di ff erence in DCMD (Fig. 10 A). As discussed in the previous paragraph, the pilot scale DCMD module is more limited by in fl ow energy and is therefore more sensitive the temperature di ff erence applied on the module.

texts.orig: Regarding the e ffi cient use of energy, a direct comparison of the lab and pilot results is di ffi cult. First of all, the energy e ffi ciency at lab scale is highly uncertain for some process conditions due to the large measurement error, as discussed in Fig. 7. Secondly, at lab scale the single pass energy e ffi ciency is measured without heat recuperation, while at large scale, the GOR is reported, which incorporates also energy recuperation.

texts.orig: At pilot scale, the GOR is stronger and negatively a ff ected by increasing the fl ow velocity in the AGMD con fi guration (Fig. 10 E). This indicates that energy recovery inside the module is more e ffi cient at lower fl ow velocities with longer residence time and lower temperature di ff erence. This e ff ect was also observed by Winter et al., where the fl ux increases and GOR decreases with increasing fl owrate [16]. This is also true for the recuperating heat exchanger in DCMD, but the DCMD
texts.text: At pilot scale, the GOR is stronger and negatively a ff ected by increasing the fl ow velocity in the AGMD con fi guration (Fig. 10 E). This indicates that energy recovery inside the module is more e ffi cient at lower fl ow velocities with longer residence time and lower temperature di ff erence. This e ff ect was also observed by Winter et al., where the fl ux increases and GOR decreases with increasing fl owrate [16]. This is also true for the recuperating heat exchanger in DCMD, but the DCMD

At high fl ow velocity, the GOR of DCMD can even become larger compared to AGMD (Fig. 10 E). texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study provides novel insights on the operation of the di ff erent con fi gurations. At lab scale, the fl ux is positively a ff ected by decreasing the salt concentration or increasing temperature di ff erence and fl ow velocity for all con fi gurations, the sensitivity of the fl ux for each parameter is found to be con fi guration dependent. DCMD is more affected by fl ow velocity than AGMD, due to the more pronounced temperature polarization in DCMD. While at lab scale, the AGMD shows 4 times lower fl uxes compared to DCMD, slightly higher fl ux and GOR are observed at pilot scale. Several reasons are described explaining this e ff ect including fi lling of the gap compartment, membrane compression and the limitation in in fl ow velocity and in fl ow energy. These fi ndings, which are in contradiction to common knowledge based on lab scale results, stress the importance of evaluating lab scale experiments with caution and performing full scale experiments to include all e ff ects in full scale modules. In general, only by combining lab and pilot results optimal con fi gurations and process conditions can be determined for each application.
